Military unit

The **United States Fleet Forces Command** (**USFFC**[[2]](#cite_note-2) or less commonly **USFLTFORCOM**[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a service component command of the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") that provides naval forces to a wide variety of U.S. forces. The naval resources may be allocated to combatant commanders such as [United States Northern Command](/wiki/United_States_Northern_Command "United States Northern Command") (USNORTHCOM) under the authority of the [secretary of defense](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Defense "United States Secretary of Defense"). Originally formed as **United States Atlantic Fleet** (**USLANTFLT**) in 1906, it has been an integral part of the defense of the United States of America since the early 20th century. In 2002, the Fleet comprised over 118,000 Navy and Marine Corps personnel serving on 186 ships and in 1,300 aircraft, with an area of responsibility ranging over most of the Atlantic Ocean from the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") to the [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole"), the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea"), [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"), and the waters of the Pacific Ocean along the coasts of [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and South America (as far west as the [Galapagos Islands](/wiki/Galapagos_Islands "Galapagos Islands")).

In 2006, the **U.S. Atlantic Fleet** was renamed to the **United States Fleet Forces Command**.

The command is based at [Naval Support Activity Hampton Roads](/wiki/Naval_Support_Activity_Hampton_Roads "Naval Support Activity Hampton Roads") in [Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia")[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) and is the Navy's service component to [U.S. Northern Command](/wiki/United_States_Northern_Command "United States Northern Command")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and is the Joint Functional Maritime Component Command under the [U.S. Strategic Command](/wiki/United_States_Strategic_Command "United States Strategic Command").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The command's mission is to organize, man, train, and equip naval forces for assignment to [unified combatant command](/wiki/Unified_combatant_command "Unified combatant command") commanders; to deter, detect, and defend against homeland maritime threats; and to articulate Fleet warfighting and readiness requirements to the [chief of naval operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Expansion and contraction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Expansion and contraction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Atlantic_Squadron_parade_Seattle_1908_B%26W.jpg/250px-Atlantic_Squadron_parade_Seattle_1908_B%26W.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_Squadron_parade_Seattle_1908_B%26W.jpg)

Atlantic Squadron parade Seattle, 1908

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/President_Theodore_Roosevelt_-_NH_1836.jpg/250px-President_Theodore_Roosevelt_-_NH_1836.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Theodore_Roosevelt_-_NH_1836.jpg)

President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") addresses crewmen on [*Connecticut*](/wiki/USS_Connecticut_(BB-18) "USS Connecticut (BB-18)"), upon her return from the Fleet's cruise around the world, 22 February 1909.

The Atlantic Fleet was established by President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") in 1906, at the same time as the [Pacific Fleet](/wiki/U.S._Pacific_Fleet "U.S. Pacific Fleet"), as protection for new bases in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") acquired as a result of the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War"). The Fleet was a combination of the [North Atlantic Fleet](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Squadron "North Atlantic Squadron") and the [South Atlantic Squadron](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Squadron "South Atlantic Squadron").

The first commander of the fleet was [Rear Admiral](/wiki/Rear_Admiral_(United_States) "Rear Admiral (United States)") [Robley D. Evans](/wiki/Robley_D._Evans_(admiral) "Robley D. Evans (admiral)"), who hoisted his flag in the [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") [USS *Maine* (BB-10)](/wiki/USS_Maine_(BB-10) "USS Maine (BB-10)") on 1 January 1906. The following year, he took his 16 battleships, now dubbed the [Great White Fleet](/wiki/Great_White_Fleet "Great White Fleet"), on a round-the-world cruise that lasted until 1909, a goodwill tour that also served the purpose of advertising the United States' naval strength and reach to all other nations of the globe.

In January 1913 the fleet consisted of six first-line divisions, a torpedo flotilla, submarines, and fleet auxiliaries.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The fleet was under the command of Rear Admiral [Hugo Osterhaus](/wiki/Hugo_Osterhaus "Hugo Osterhaus").

* The First Division, under Rear Admiral Bradley A. Fiske, consisted of [USS *Florida* (BB-30)](/wiki/USS_Florida_(BB-30) "USS Florida (BB-30)") (flag), [USS *Delaware* (BB-28)](/wiki/USS_Delaware_(BB-28) "USS Delaware (BB-28)"), and [USS *North Dakota* (BB-29)](/wiki/USS_North_Dakota_(BB-29) "USS North Dakota (BB-29)").
* The Second Division, under Rear Admiral Nathaniel R. Usher with his flag aboard the [USS *Vermont* (BB-20)](/wiki/USS_Vermont_(BB-20) "USS Vermont (BB-20)"), consisted of [USS *Louisiana* (BB-19)](/wiki/USS_Louisiana_(BB-19) "USS Louisiana (BB-19)"), [USS *Michigan* (BB-27)](/wiki/USS_Michigan_(BB-27) "USS Michigan (BB-27)"), [USS *New Hampshire* (BB-25)](/wiki/USS_New_Hampshire_(BB-25) "USS New Hampshire (BB-25)"), and [USS *South Carolina* (BB-26)](/wiki/USS_South_Carolina_(BB-26) "USS South Carolina (BB-26)").
* The Third Division, under Rear Admiral Cameron McR. Winslow, comprised [USS *Virginia* (BB-13)](/wiki/USS_Virginia_(BB-13) "USS Virginia (BB-13)") (flag), [USS *Georgia* (BB-15)](/wiki/USS_Georgia_(BB-15) "USS Georgia (BB-15)"), [USS *New Jersey* (BB-16)](/wiki/USS_New_Jersey_(BB-16) "USS New Jersey (BB-16)"), [USS *Rhode Island* (BB-17)](/wiki/USS_Rhode_Island_(BB-17) "USS Rhode Island (BB-17)"), and [USS *Nebraska* (BB-14)](/wiki/USS_Nebraska_(BB-14) "USS Nebraska (BB-14)").
* The Fourth Division, under Rear Admiral [Frank F. Fletcher](/wiki/Frank_Friday_Fletcher "Frank Friday Fletcher"), consisted of the [USS *Minnesota* (BB-22)](/wiki/USS_Minnesota_(BB-22) "USS Minnesota (BB-22)"), [USS *Connecticut* (BB-18)](/wiki/USS_Connecticut_(BB-18) "USS Connecticut (BB-18)"), [USS *Ohio* (BB-12)](/wiki/USS_Ohio_(BB-12) "USS Ohio (BB-12)"), [USS *Idaho* (BB-24)](/wiki/Greek_battleship_Lemnos "Greek battleship Lemnos"), and [USS *Kansas* (BB-21)](/wiki/USS_Kansas_(BB-21) "USS Kansas (BB-21)"). (See [United States occupation of Veracruz](/wiki/United_States_occupation_of_Veracruz "United States occupation of Veracruz")).
* Fifth and Sixth Divisions were made up of protected cruisers, [USS *St. Louis* (C-20)](/wiki/USS_St._Louis_(C-20) "USS St. Louis (C-20)"), [USS *Tennessee* (ACR-10)](/wiki/USS_Tennessee_(ACR-10) "USS Tennessee (ACR-10)"), [USS *Washington* (ACR-11)](/wiki/USS_Washington_(ACR-11) "USS Washington (ACR-11)"), and [USS *Cleveland* (C-19)](/wiki/USS_Cleveland_(C-19) "USS Cleveland (C-19)"), [USS *Denver* (CL-16)](/wiki/USS_Denver_(CL-16) "USS Denver (CL-16)"), [USS *Des Moines* (CL-17)](/wiki/USS_Des_Moines_(CL-17) "USS Des Moines (CL-17)"), and [USS *Tacoma* (CL-20)](/wiki/USS_Tacoma_(CL-20) "USS Tacoma (CL-20)").

The [Cruiser and Transport Force](/wiki/Cruiser_and_Transport_Force "Cruiser and Transport Force"), under Rear Admiral [Albert Gleaves](/wiki/Albert_Gleaves "Albert Gleaves") served in Atlantic waters during World War I moving the [American Expeditionary Forces](/wiki/American_Expeditionary_Forces "American Expeditionary Forces") to Europe. [United States Battleship Division Nine](/wiki/United_States_Battleship_Division_Nine "United States Battleship Division Nine") joined the [Grand Fleet](/wiki/Grand_Fleet "Grand Fleet") in the UK.

The Atlantic Fleet was reorganized into the [Scouting Force](/wiki/Scouting_Force "Scouting Force") in 1923, which was under the [United States Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Fleet "United States Fleet") along with the Pacific Fleet. In January 1939 the Atlantic Squadron, [United States Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Fleet "United States Fleet"), was formed,[[11]](#cite_note-ShortBattle-11) with Vice Admiral [Alfred Wilkinson Johnson](/wiki/Alfred_Wilkinson_Johnson "Alfred Wilkinson Johnson") commanded.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The aircraft carrier [USS *Ranger* (CV-4)](/wiki/USS_Ranger_(CV-4) "USS Ranger (CV-4)") was transferred to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), to join three [battleships](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship").

On 1 November 1940 the Atlantic Squadron was renamed the Patrol Force. The Patrol Force was organized into type commands: Battleships, Patrol Force; Cruisers, Patrol Force; Destroyers, Patrol Force; and, Train, Patrol Force (the logistics arms).[[11]](#cite_note-ShortBattle-11)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: World War II")]

On 1 February 1941, the Atlantic Fleet was resurrected and organized from the [Patrol Force](/wiki/Patrol_Force "Patrol Force"). Along with the Pacific Fleet and [Asiatic Fleet](/wiki/Asiatic_Fleet "Asiatic Fleet"), the fleet was to be under the command of a full admiral, which jumped the fleet's commander [Ernest J. King](/wiki/Ernest_J._King "Ernest J. King") from a two-star to a four-star. King's [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") was [USS *Texas* (BB-35)](/wiki/USS_Texas_(BB-35) "USS Texas (BB-35)").

Subsequently, the headquarters was in a rather odd assortment of ships; the [USS *Augusta* (CA-31)](/wiki/USS_Augusta_(CA-31) "USS Augusta (CA-31)"), then the old wooden ship [USS *Constellation*](/wiki/USS_Constellation_(1854) "USS Constellation (1854)"), [USS *Vixen* (PG-53)](/wiki/USS_Vixen_(PG-53) "USS Vixen (PG-53)"), and then [USS *Pocono* (AGC-16)](/wiki/USS_Pocono "USS Pocono"). In 1948, the HQ moved into the former naval hospital at [Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia"), and has remained there ever since.

In July 1942, eight months after the United States entered the war, the Commander-in-Chief of the Royal Navy's [America and West Indies Station](/wiki/North_America_and_West_Indies_Station "North America and West Indies Station") based at [Admiralty House, Bermuda](/wiki/Admiralty_House,_Bermuda "Admiralty House, Bermuda") had his title changed to *Senior British Naval Officer, Western Atlantic.* [USS *Augusta*](/wiki/USS_Augusta "USS Augusta") visited Bermuda in September, 1941.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

##### Other elements of the Atlantic Fleet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other elements of the Atlantic Fleet")]

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") "Transports, Amphibious Force, Atlantic Fleet" was part of this command (ComTransPhibLant). Smaller units included the Antisubmarine Development Detachment, Atlantic Fleet (ASDEVLANT) located at [Quonset Point, Rhode Island](/wiki/Quonset_Point,_Rhode_Island "Quonset Point, Rhode Island").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The detachment was responsible for the study and development of [antisubmarine](/wiki/Antisubmarine "Antisubmarine") gear during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The Commander of the detachment was known as COMASDEVLANT.

Admiral King was appointed Commander-in-Chief, United States Fleet, on 20 December 1941. Rear Admiral [Royal E. Ingersoll](/wiki/Royal_E._Ingersoll "Royal E. Ingersoll") was designated, with the rank of vice admiral, to relieve him as Commander-in-Chief, Atlantic Fleet.[[16]](#cite_note-16) He took command on 1 January 1942, and was advanced to the rank of admiral on 1 July 1942. To carry out this mission and other tasks CinCLant had in the meantime been reorganized, as of 1 March 1941, into ten task forces (commanded by flag officers) numbered from one to ten and named according to their intended employment. [Task Force One](/wiki/Task_Force_One "Task Force One") was the Ocean Escort Force, TF2—Striking Force, TF3—Scouting Force, TF4—Support Force, TF5—Submarine Force, TF6—Naval Coastal Frontier Forces, TF7—Bermuda Force, TF8—Patrol Wings, TF9—Service Force, and [Task Force 10](/w/index.php?title=Task_Force_10&action=edit&redlink=1 "Task Force 10 (page does not exist)"), [1st Marine Division](/wiki/1st_Marine_Division "1st Marine Division") (commanded by a Brigadier General).

Of many significant engagements, [Captain Daniel Gallery](/wiki/Daniel_V._Gallery "Daniel V. Gallery")'s capture of the [German submarine U-505](/wiki/German_submarine_U-505 "German submarine U-505") stands out. The capture was so top secret (because of the enigma code books captured) that the ship's flag was kept by the Commander-in-Chief, Atlantic Fleet, and not handed over to Navy authorities until after the war.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cold War")]

On 1 January 1946, Commander Minesweeping Forces, Atlantic Fleet (ComMinLant) was activated to command minesweepers assigned to the Atlantic Fleet. The Commander, Mine Forces, Atlantic was responsible for all Fleet mine warfare operations. Units under his command were divided into Minesweeping Squadrons (MineRon)s.

Between 1947 and 1985, the fleet commander's post was a concurrent appointment with the Commander-in-Chief of [United States Atlantic Command](/wiki/United_States_Atlantic_Command "United States Atlantic Command"). The [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief "Commander-in-Chief") Atlantic Fleet (CINCLANTFLT) was traditionally a navy four-star admiral who also then held the positions of Commander-in-Chief [United States Atlantic Command](/wiki/United_States_Atlantic_Command "United States Atlantic Command") (CINCLANT) and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")'s [Supreme Allied Commander Atlantic](/wiki/Supreme_Allied_Commander_Atlantic "Supreme Allied Commander Atlantic") (SACLANT). But after a major reorganization of the U.S. armed forces structure following the [Goldwater-Nichols Act](/wiki/Goldwater-Nichols_Act "Goldwater-Nichols Act") of 1986, CINCLANFLT was separated from the two other billets. The admiral commanding the Atlantic Fleet was designated as the Deputy Commander in Chief of the Atlantic Command until 1986.

Major crises the Atlantic Fleet was involved in during the Cold War included the 1962 [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") and the [1965 United States occupation of the Dominican Republic](/wiki/1965_United_States_occupation_of_the_Dominican_Republic "1965 United States occupation of the Dominican Republic").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") meant that from 16 October 1962 a temporary command organization, for the newly required forces was necessary. The Commander in Chief, Atlantic Command (CINCLANT), Admiral [Robert Dennison](/wiki/Robert_Dennison_(US_Navy_officer) "Robert Dennison (US Navy officer)"), became the unified commander. He also retained control of all naval components involved in tactical operations, as the Commander-in-Chief, Atlantic Fleet. The responsibility for Army and Air Force components was assigned to the Continental Army Command (CONARC) and the [Tactical Air Command](/wiki/Tactical_Air_Command "Tactical Air Command") under the designation of Army Forces, Atlantic (ARLANT), and Air Forces, Atlantic (AFLANT). The commander of the Army [XVIII Airborne Corps](/wiki/XVIII_Airborne_Corps "XVIII Airborne Corps") was designated Joint Task Force Commander to plan for any joint operations that might become necessary. Over-all direction was exercised by the President and the Secretary of Defense through the Joint Chiefs of Staff, who named the [Chief of Naval Operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations") as their representative for the quarantine.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)

Major elements of the [Strategic Army Corps](/wiki/Strategic_Army_Corps "Strategic Army Corps") were designated for use by ARLANT and placed in advanced alert status. Logistic support for the more than 100,000 men involved was directed by a newly established Peninsula Base Command. Preparatory steps were taken to make possible the immediate callup of high priority [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") and [Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve") units. [Tactical Air Command](/wiki/Tactical_Air_Command "Tactical Air Command") moved hundreds of tactical fighter, reconnaissance, and troop carrier aircraft to the southeast. To make room for all these units, the bombers, tankers, and other aircraft not required for the current operations were ordered to other bases in the United States.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)

From the late 1960s, nuclear ballistic missile submarines of the fleet began to make thousands of deterrent patrols.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The first patrol in the Atlantic Fleet [area of operations](/wiki/Area_of_operations "Area of operations") was made by [USS *George Washington* (SSBN-598)](/wiki/USS_George_Washington_(SSBN-598) "USS George Washington (SSBN-598)").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1972, Commander, Anti-Submarine Warfare Force, Atlantic Fleet (Task Force 81) was headquartered at [Quonset Point Naval Air Station](/wiki/Quonset_Point_Naval_Air_Station "Quonset Point Naval Air Station").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Under ASWFORLANTFLT was Hunter-Killer Force, Atlantic Fleet (Task Force 83), with [Carrier Divisions 14](/w/index.php?title=Carrier_Division_14&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carrier Division 14 (page does not exist)") and [16](/w/index.php?title=Carrier_Division_16&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carrier Division 16 (page does not exist)") (Wasp and Intrepid, respectively), as well as the Quonset ASW Group (TG 81.2) with [Fleet Air Wing 3](/w/index.php?title=Fleet_Air_Wing_3&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fleet Air Wing 3 (page does not exist)") and surface units. More information on Anti-Submarine Warfare Force, Atlantic Fleet's, activities during the Cuban crisis can be found at the National Security Archive's document collections.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The [Commander, Naval Surface Force Atlantic](/wiki/Commander,_Naval_Surface_Force_Atlantic "Commander, Naval Surface Force Atlantic") was formed on 1 July 1975, incorporating a number of previously separate smaller commands – mine warfare vessels/units, service vessels, and frigates, destroyers and cruisers, along with associated destroyer squadrons and cruiser/destroyer groups.

As part of a reorganization announced in July 1995 of the Atlantic Fleet's surface combatant ships into six core battle groups, nine destroyer squadrons, and a new Western Hemisphere Group, [USS *John Hancock* (DD-981)](/wiki/USS_John_Hancock_(DD-981) "USS John Hancock (DD-981)") was reassigned to [Destroyer Squadron 24](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer_Squadron_24&action=edit&redlink=1 "Destroyer Squadron 24 (page does not exist)"). The re-organization was to be phased in over the summer and take effect 31 August 1995, with homeport shifts occurring through 1998. In September 1995 the following ship assignments were intended to apply at the end of the transitional period:[[24]](#cite_note-24)

* [Western Hemisphere Group](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere_Group "Western Hemisphere Group") (to be homeported at [Naval Station Pascagoula](/wiki/Naval_Station_Pascagoula "Naval Station Pascagoula") and [Naval Station Mayport](/wiki/Naval_Station_Mayport "Naval Station Mayport")): [USS *Ticonderoga* (CG-47)](/wiki/USS_Ticonderoga_(CG-47) "USS Ticonderoga (CG-47)"), [USS *Yorktown* (CG-48)](/wiki/USS_Yorktown_(CG-48) "USS Yorktown (CG-48)"), [USS *Thomas S. Gates* (CG-51)](/wiki/USS_Thomas_S._Gates "USS Thomas S. Gates") (to move to Pascagoula in FY 98), [USS *Robert G. Bradley* (FFG-49)](/wiki/USS_Robert_G._Bradley "USS Robert G. Bradley"), *Conolly*, *Scott,* DDG-993, *Moosebrugger*, *Dewert*, *McInerney*, *Boone*, *Doyle*, *Aubrey Fitch* and *Stark.*

* [Carrier Group 2](/wiki/Carrier_Strike_Group_Two "Carrier Strike Group Two")/Stennis Joint Task Group: [USS *San Jacinto* (CG-56)](/wiki/USS_San_Jacinto_(CG-56) "USS San Jacinto (CG-56)"), [USS *Monterey* (CG-61)](/wiki/USS_Monterey_(CG-61) "USS Monterey (CG-61)")

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In February 2000, [U.S. Naval Forces Southern Command](/wiki/U.S._Naval_Forces_Southern_Command "U.S. Naval Forces Southern Command") was established in [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), and the Western Hemisphere Group became [Naval Surface Group 2](/w/index.php?title=Naval_Surface_Group_2&action=edit&redlink=1 "Naval Surface Group 2 (page does not exist)").

After the [September 11 terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11_terrorist_attacks "September 11 terrorist attacks"), the Atlantic Fleet sent aircraft carriers and cruisers towards New York, on the fleet commander's own initiative.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Admiral Natter quickly ordered Aegis guided missile cruisers up Chesapeake Bay to provide an anti-aircraft shield over Washington DC, and USS George Washington (CVN 73) to New York with a hastily sortied complement of fighter aircraft.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On 1 October 2001, the [Chief of Naval Operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations") designated Commander-in-Chief, Atlantic Fleet (CINCLANTFLT) as concurrent Commander, **Fleet Forces Command** (CFFC). In October–November 2002, the title of Commander in Chief, Atlantic Fleet was amended to Commander, U.S. Atlantic Fleet (COMLANTFLT).

In the CNO Guidance for 2003, Admiral Vernon Clark stipulated that the terms [Carrier Battle Group](/wiki/Carrier_Battle_Group "Carrier Battle Group") and [Amphibious ready group](/wiki/Amphibious_ready_group "Amphibious ready group") would be replaced by [Carrier Strike Groups](/wiki/Carrier_Strike_Group "Carrier Strike Group") (CSG) and [Expeditionary Strike Groups](/wiki/Expeditionary_Strike_Group "Expeditionary Strike Group") (ESGs), respectively, by March 2003. Cruiser-Destroyer and Carrier Groups (CARGRU) were also redesignated, as Carrier Strike Groups (CSG), and aligned directly under the numbered fleet commanders. The two sets of staffs were formerly under the administrative authority of their respective air and surface [U.S. Navy type commands](/wiki/U.S._Navy_type_commands "U.S. Navy type commands"). This realignment allowed key operational leaders authority and direct access to the personnel required to more effectively accomplish the navy's mission.

The numbered fleet commanders are now responsible for the training and certification of the entire Strike Group. The organizational structure to support the carrier strike groups focuses more on placing Strike Group commanders under the authority of the certifying officer, or the numbered fleet commander. Under this new division of responsibility, the air-side type commander gains authority over the air wing, and the surface-side type commander gains authority over the carrier itself and the rest of the ships of the battle group.

On 23 May 2006, the Chief of Naval Operations renamed COMLANTFLT to Commander, **U.S. Fleet Forces Command** (COMUSFLTFORCOM or CUSFFC), ordered to carry out the missions currently performed by COMFLTFORCOM (CFFC) and serve as primary advocate for fleet personnel, training, requirements, maintenance, and operational issues, reporting administratively directly to the CNO as an Echelon 2 command. The previous title CFFC was disestablished at the same time.[[27]](#cite_note-27) CUSFFC previously served as the Naval component of the [US Joint Forces Command](/wiki/United_States_Joint_Forces_Command "United States Joint Forces Command") ([USJFCOM](/wiki/USJFCOM "USJFCOM")) until the disestablishment of USJFCOM in August 2011. CFFC is also assigned as the supporting service component commander to Commander, [United States Northern Command](/wiki/United_States_Northern_Command "United States Northern Command") (USNORTHCOM) as well as to Commander, [United States Strategic Command](/wiki/United_States_Strategic_Command "United States Strategic Command") (USSTRATCOM).

*Enterprise* entered an ESRA in 2008, but the refit took longer than expected. Thus on 11 September 2009, it was announced that the carrier strike group deployment schedule would be changed to accommodate the delay in the return of the *Enterprise* from its current overhaul. This resulted in extending both [Carrier Strike Group Eleven](/wiki/Carrier_Strike_Group_Eleven "Carrier Strike Group Eleven")'s 2009–2010 deployment and [Carrier Strike Group Ten](/wiki/Carrier_Strike_Group_Ten "Carrier Strike Group Ten")'s 2010 deployment to eight months.[[28]](#cite_note-28) *Enterprise* returned to Naval Station Norfolk on 19 April 2010 after completing its post-overhaul sea trials, signifying the beginning of its pre-deployment training cycle.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

On 24 July 2009, Admiral [John C. Harvey, Jr.](/wiki/John_C._Harvey,_Jr. "John C. Harvey, Jr.") relieved Admiral [Jonathan W. Greenert](/wiki/Jonathan_W._Greenert "Jonathan W. Greenert") as Commander.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2010s")]

News reports in July 2011 said that in connection with the disestablishment of the [United States Second Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Second_Fleet "United States Second Fleet"), Fleet Forces Command would take over Second Fleet's duties on 30 September 2011.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Effectively this meant [Task Force 20](/wiki/Task_Force_20 "Task Force 20") (TF 20), under a deputy commander of the fleet, took over that mission. Task Force 20 was succeeded by [Task Force 80](/wiki/Task_Force_80 "Task Force 80") effective 1 October 2012, with TF-80 being under the command of the director of the Maritime Headquarters, Fleet Forces Command.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The [Fleet Numerical Meteorology and Oceanography Center](/wiki/Fleet_Numerical_Meteorology_and_Oceanography_Center "Fleet Numerical Meteorology and Oceanography Center") (FNMOC), [United States Naval Observatory](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Observatory "United States Naval Observatory") (USNO), [Naval Oceanographic Office](/wiki/Naval_Oceanographic_Office "Naval Oceanographic Office") (NAVOCEANO), Naval Oceanography Operations Command, Naval Meteorology and Oceanography Professional Development Center, were all realigned under [U.S. Navy Information Dominance Forces](/wiki/U.S._Navy_Information_Dominance_Forces "U.S. Navy Information Dominance Forces") on 1 October 2014.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Beginning in Fiscal Year 2015, the Optimized Fleet Response Plan will align carrier strike groups to a 36-month training and deployment cycle. All required maintenance, training, evaluations, plus a single eight-month overseas deployment, are scheduled throughout this 36-month cycle in order to reduce costs while increasing overall fleet readiness. This new plan streamlined the inspection and evaluation process while maintaining a surge capacity for emergency deployments. The ultimate objective is to reduce time at sea while increasing in-port time from 49% to 68%. While initially to be used by U.S. Navy carrier strike groups, the Optimized Fleet Response Plan will be adopted for all fleet operations.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Accordingly, the carrier [USS *Harry S. Truman* (CVN-75)](/wiki/USS_Harry_S._Truman "USS Harry S. Truman") will be the first carrier to deploy under this new O-FRP cycle, replacing the previously scheduled *Eisenhower* in the deployment lineup. Additionally, the Carrier Strike Group Eight command staff will deploy with the *Truman* while the *Eisenhower* will serve as the new flagship for [Carrier Strike Group Ten](/wiki/Carrier_Strike_Group_Ten "Carrier Strike Group Ten").

On 2 December 2020, Secretary [Kenneth Braithwaite](/wiki/Kenneth_Braithwaite "Kenneth Braithwaite") announced that U.S. Fleet Forces Command will be renamed back to **United States Atlantic Fleet**[[34]](#cite_note-34) to focus more on the growing maritime threats coming from the Atlantic.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-rename-36) The renaming of the command has been placed on hold, pending further review of the U.S. military footprint, resources, strategy and missions, from the global force posture review.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Structure 2013

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Structure 2013")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. The reason given is: **This should be updated to the recent structure here <https://www.usff.navy.mil/>**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2026)* |

Further information: [Structure of the United States Navy](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Navy "Structure of the United States Navy")

In accordance with the *Navigation Plan 2013–2017* guidance from the [Chief of Naval Operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations"), U.S. Fleet Forces Command was to be based upon the three tenets of war-fighting, forward operations, and readiness.[[38]](#cite_note-Gortney-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) To achieve these objectives, Fleet Forces Command was realigned to a Maritime Operations Center (MOC) and Maritime Headquarters (MHQ) command structure. Additionally, the Commander, U.S. Fleet Forces Command (COMUSFLTFORCOM) is designated as the Joint Forces Maritime Component Commander North (JFMCC-N) to the [U.S. Northern Command](/wiki/U.S._Northern_Command "U.S. Northern Command").[[38]](#cite_note-Gortney-38) Joint Forces Maritime Component Commander North consists of two Maritime Command Elements (MCE), with Maritime Command Element-East (MCE-E) being [Task Force 180](/wiki/Task_Force_180 "Task Force 180") and Maritime Command Element-West (MCE-W) provided from units assigned to the [U.S. Pacific Fleet](/wiki/U.S._Pacific_Fleet "U.S. Pacific Fleet").[[38]](#cite_note-Gortney-38)[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40)

Effective 17 May 2013, Commander, U.S. Fleet Forces Command was officially designated as the naval component commander for the U.S. Northern Command.[[41]](#cite_note-DNS-33/13U102246-41) In this new capacity, the Commander, U.S. Fleet Forces Command is to contribute to the defense of North America through the coordination, collaboration, and communication with allied, coalition, and joint forces within the U.S. Northern Command's [area of responsibility](/wiki/Area_of_responsibility "Area of responsibility").[[41]](#cite_note-DNS-33/13U102246-41) Under this reorganization, the [Commander, Navy Installations Command](/wiki/Commander,_Navy_Installations_Command_(United_States) "Commander, Navy Installations Command (United States)") is responsible for area coordination for U.S. Naval Forces Northern Command.[[41]](#cite_note-DNS-33/13U102246-41) Additionally, Commander, [Navy Region Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Navy_Region_Mid-Atlantic "Navy Region Mid-Atlantic") is responsible for regional coordination for U.S. Naval Forces Northern Command.[[41]](#cite_note-DNS-33/13U102246-41)

### Maritime Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Maritime Operations")]

The Maritime Operations directorate leads all phases of the pre-deployment fleet response training plan (FRTP) cycle involving those naval units assigned to the Fleet Forces Command. The directorate transitions all naval units from their operational phase to their tactical phase prior to their overseas deployment.[[38]](#cite_note-Gortney-38)[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40)

The Director of Maritime Operations (DMO) is an active-duty two-star [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States)#Rear_admiral "Rear admiral (United States)") in the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/U.S._Navy "U.S. Navy") while the Deputy Director of Maritime Operations is a one-star [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States)#Rear_admiral_(lower_half) "Rear admiral (United States)") from the [United States Naval Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Reserve "United States Naval Reserve").[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40) As of 2013, the DMO was Rear Admiral Dan Cloyd. Maritime Operations is organized into the following directorates:[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40)

* **N2/39** – Intelligence and Information Warfare
* **N3/N5** – Joint / Fleet Operations
  + **N31** – Maritime Operations Center (MOC)
* **N041** – Global Force Management
* **N042** – Force Protection
* **N7** – Joint / Fleet Training

### Maritime Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Maritime Headquarters")]

The Maritime Headquarters (MHQ) leads all phases prior to the pre-deployment training cycle, including resourcing, policy development, assessment, procurement, and pre-introduction of naval units assigned to the Fleet Forces Command. The MHQ transitions all naval units from their strategical phase to their operational phase prior to their pre-deployment training cycle, and in the capacity, it supports the Maritime Operations Center.[[38]](#cite_note-Gortney-38)[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40) The Director of Maritime Headquarters (DMHQ) is an active-duty two-star [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States)#Rear_admiral "Rear admiral (United States)") in the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/U.S._Navy "U.S. Navy") while the Deputy Director of Maritime Headquarters is a one-star [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States)#Rear_admiral_(lower_half) "Rear admiral (United States)") from the [United States Naval Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Reserve "United States Naval Reserve").[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40) As of July 2013, the DMHQ was Rear Admiral [Bradley R. Gehrke](/w/index.php?title=Bradley_R._Gehrke&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bradley R. Gehrke (page does not exist)").[[42]](#cite_note-42) The Maritime Headquarters is organized into the following directorates:[[40]](#cite_note-Guidance-40)

* **N1** – Fleet Personnel Development and Allocation (including information architecture management and Navy Security Forces)
* **N41** – Fleet Ordnance and Supply
* **N43** – Fleet Maintenance
* **N45/46** – Fleet Installations and Environment
* **N6** – Fleet Communications and Information Systems
* **N8/N9** – Fleet Capabilities, Requirements, Concepts, and Experimentation (including missile defense)
* **N03FS** – Fleet Safety and Occupational Health
* **N03G** – Fleet Religious Ministries
* **N03H** – Fleet Surgeon and Health Services
* **N03M** – Fleet Marine

U.S. Fleet Forces Subordinate Commands include the following:[[43]](#cite_note-links-43)

  + Navy Munitions Command (NMC)

### Type commands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Type commands")]

Main article: [U.S. Navy type commands](/wiki/U.S._Navy_type_commands "U.S. Navy type commands")

All ships are organized into categories by type. Aircraft carriers, aircraft squadrons, and air stations are under the administrative control of the appropriate Commander Naval Air Force. Submarines come under the Commander Submarine Force. All other ships fall under Commander Naval Surface Force. Type commands for Fleet Forces Command include:

* [Naval Air Force U.S. Atlantic Fleet (AIRLANT)](/wiki/Commander,_Naval_Air_Force_U.S._Atlantic_Fleet "Commander, Naval Air Force U.S. Atlantic Fleet")
* [Submarine Force U.S. Atlantic Fleet (SUBLANT)](/wiki/ComSubLant "ComSubLant")
* [Naval Surface Force Atlantic (NAVSURFLANT)](/wiki/Commander,_Naval_Surface_Force_Atlantic "Commander, Naval Surface Force Atlantic")
* [Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC)](/wiki/Navy_Expeditionary_Combat_Command "Navy Expeditionary Combat Command")
* [Naval Information Forces (NAVIFOR)](/wiki/U.S._Naval_Information_Forces "U.S. Naval Information Forces")
